steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which psychologist's quotation would most directly weaken the team's conclusion. The conclusion is that the ability to distinguish spontaneous from volitional laughter “appears to be universal across cultures.” That conclusion rests on the assumption that the two types of laughter are acoustically distinct enough to tell apart. A statement showing they are not reliably distinct would undermine it.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘"When an individual chooses to produce volitional laughter in a natural social context, the laughter often shares certain acoustic qualities, such as pitch and fluctuation of intensity, with spontaneous laughter."’: If volitional laughter shares key acoustic properties with spontaneous laughter in real social contexts, participants may not actually be detecting a genuine difference — the discrimination task becomes unreliable, undermining the universal-ability claim. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘"Although the team considered the average size of communities in each society in the study, that demographic factor was found to have no effect on listeners' identifications of laughter as spontaneous or volitional."’: This rules out a demographic confound and actually supports the universality conclusion by showing the result held regardless of community size. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘"Judgments of spontaneous laughter are often associated with acoustic features such as greater intensity variability and higher pitch."’: This identifies distinguishing acoustic markers, which supports the idea that listeners can differentiate the two types. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘"Recent studies in communications have shown that certain acoustic features of spontaneous laughter, such as pitch and intensity, are consistent both within and across societies."’: Consistency across societies would support cross-cultural recognition, not weaken it. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
